title: What to know before using Evodia rutaecarpa (Evodia)?
medicine_intro: Evodia rutaecarpa has several medicinal uses, such as treating digestive disorders, reducing pain, and preventing inflammation. Its constituents include evodiamine, rutaecarpine, and limonin, which have been shown to have various pharmacological activities, such as anti-cancer, anti-inflammatory, and anti-obesity effects. Preparations of evodia can be made from its fruit, which can be dried and ground into a powder, or extracted with solvents to produce a liquid extract. However, it is important to note that evodia can cause side effects such as nausea, vomiting, and diarrhea, especially when taken in large doses. Therefore, it is recommended to consult with a healthcare professional before taking evodia and to follow the recommended dosage instructions carefully. Additionally, pregnant women and individuals with certain medical conditions, such as gastrointestinal disorders or heart disease, should avoid using evodia due to potential adverse effects.
botany_desc: Evodia rutaecarpa, also known as Evodia officinalis or Wu Zhu Yu in Chinese, is a species of plant in the Rutaceae family. It is native to China, specifically in the provinces of Anhui, Fujian, Gansu, Guangdong, Guangxi, Guizhou, Henan, Hubei, Hunan, Jiangsu, Jiangxi, Sichuan, and Zhejiang. This plant is commonly found in forests, thickets, and valleys at elevations ranging from 200 to 2000 meters.

Morphologically, Evodia rutaecarpa is a deciduous tree or shrub that can grow up to 10 meters tall. Its leaves are alternate, ovate-oblong, and 5-12 cm long, with a serrated margin. The leaves are dark green and glossy on the upper surface, while the lower surface is light green and pubescent. The flowers are small, white, and appear in clusters in the leaf axils. The fruit is a drupe, ovoid-oblong, and 8-12 mm long, turning from green to red to black when ripe.

There are several variant names for Evodia rutaecarpa, including E. rutaecarpa var. bodinieri, E. rutaecarpa var. officinalis, and E. rutaecarpa var. verrucosa. The differences between these variants are mainly in the size and shape of the leaves, as well as the texture and color of the fruit. For example, E. rutaecarpa var. bodinieri has larger leaves and a more spreading habit than the other variants, while E. rutaecarpa var. verrucosa has a more rugose fruit surface.

Evodia rutaecarpa is native to central and southern China, where it grows in a variety of habitats, including forests, thickets, and valleys. It prefers well-drained, fertile soils and partial shade. It is often found in association with other tree species, such as Quercus, Castanopsis, and Schima. In its natural habitat, Evodia rutaecarpa provides important ecological functions, such as habitat for wildlife, soil erosion control, and carbon sequestration.

The life cycle of Evodia rutaecarpa begins with germination of the seed, which occurs in the spring. The seedlings grow slowly at first, but eventually develop into mature trees. Flowering occurs in the summer, and the fruit ripens in the fall. The fruit is eaten by birds and other animals, which disperse the seeds in their droppings. The seeds can remain viable in the soil for several years, allowing the plant to establish new colonies. In cultivation, Evodia rutaecarpa can be propagated by seed, cuttings, or layering. It is a hardy plant that can tolerate cold temperatures and drought, making it a popular ornamental tree in many parts of the world.
horticulture_desc: Evodia rutaecarpa, also known as Evodia fruit or Wu Zhu Yu, is a slow-growing shrub or small tree that is native to China. It is a popular ornamental plant in landscaping and is also cultivated for its medicinal properties. This plant prefers full sun and well-drained soil. It can tolerate a wide range of soil pH levels, from acidic to alkaline. However, it does not tolerate wet or waterlogged soils. Therefore, it is important to ensure that the plant is grown in a location with good drainage.

When it comes to planting Evodia rutaecarpa, it is best to plant it in the spring or fall. This will give the plant plenty of time to establish itself before the hot summer months or the cold winter months. When planting, dig a hole that is twice as wide as the root ball and just as deep. Place the plant in the hole and backfill with soil, being careful not to overcompact the soil. Water thoroughly after planting.

Caring for Evodia rutaecarpa is relatively easy. It is a drought-tolerant plant, but it will benefit from regular watering during the growing season. Fertilize the plant in the spring with a balanced fertilizer. Prune the plant in the late winter or early spring to control its size and shape. It is also a good idea to remove any dead or damaged branches throughout the growing season.

Harvesting Evodia rutaecarpa fruit is typically done in the fall, when the fruit turns from green to yellow. The fruit is ripe when it is soft to the touch. To harvest, simply snip the fruit from the branches with a pair of pruning shears. Be sure to wear gloves when harvesting, as the fruit can be messy and staining.

Like any other plant, Evodia rutaecarpa is susceptible to pests and diseases. Common pests include aphids, scale, and mealybugs. These pests can be controlled with insecticidal soap or horticultural oil. Diseases such as leaf spot and powdery mildew can be controlled by keeping the plant's foliage dry and providing good air circulation. It is also important to keep the plant's leaves and fruit picked up from the ground to prevent the spread of disease. Regularly inspecting the plant for signs of pests and diseases will help to keep it healthy and thriving.
intro_desc: Evodia rutaecarpa, also known as Wu Zhu Yu in traditional Chinese medicine, is a small, deciduous tree native to China. It is known for its medicinal properties, which include the ability to alleviate digestive issues such as nausea, vomiting, and abdominal pain. The tree thrives in well-drained soil and full sun, and can grow up to 20 feet tall. Its botanical characteristics include simple, ovate leaves and small, greenish-white flowers that bloom in the summer. The fruit of the tree, a red, drupel-like structure, is also used in traditional medicine.
benefits_desc: Evodia rutaecarpa has many health benefits, such as its ability to alleviate symptoms of gastrointestinal disorders. For instance, evodia has been shown to reduce inflammation and discomfort in the digestive tract, making it a potential remedy for issues like gastric ulcers and acid reflux. Additionally, evodia has been used in traditional Chinese medicine to treat nausea and vomiting, suggesting that it may have anti-emetic properties. Furthermore, evodia has been found to stimulate the production of blood cells and improve circulation, which can help boost the immune system and promote overall health. Lastly, some research suggests that evodia may have neuroprotective effects, potentially making it useful in the prevention and treatment of neurological disorders.
constituents_desc: Evodia rutaecarpa has many active constituents, such as evodiamine and rutaecarpine, which have been studied for their medicinal properties. Evodiamine is an alkaloid that has been shown to have anti-obesity, anti-inflammatory, and analgesic effects. It works by inhibiting the absorption of fat and increasing the metabolic rate, as well as by reducing inflammation and blocking pain signals in the body. Rutaecarpine, another alkaloid present in evodia, has been found to have a positive effect on cardiovascular health. It works by dilating blood vessels and increasing blood flow, which can help to lower blood pressure and improve overall cardiovascular function. Additionally, rutaecarpine has been shown to have a protective effect on the liver, and may help to reduce damage caused by alcohol and other toxins. Other constituents of evodia, such as limonin and nomilin, also have potential medicinal benefits, including anti-cancer and anti-viral properties. However, more research is needed to fully understand the mechanisms of these compounds and their potential therapeutic applications.
preparations_desc: Evodia rutaecarpa has many medicinal preparations, such as decoctions, extracts, and powders, which are used primarily in traditional Chinese medicine. The fruit of the plant is typically used to create these preparations. One common use of evodia preparations is to treat gastrointestinal disorders, including stomach pain, nausea, and diarrhea. Additionally, evodia preparations are also used to alleviate symptoms of cold and flu, such as headaches and nasal congestion. The dried and crushed fruits of evodia are often made into a powder and used in encapsulated form as a dietary supplement. Furthermore, evodia preparations can also be found in various forms, including teas, tinctures, and topical ointments, in health food stores and Asian markets.
side_effects_desc: Evodia rutaecarpa can have some side effects if used improperly, such as stomach upset and digestive discomfort, which may include symptoms like nausea, vomiting, and diarrhea. In some cases, it may also cause dry mouth, dizziness, and skin irritation. Additionally, high doses of evodia may lead to more severe side effects, such as seizures, paralysis, and even death. It's also worth noting that evodia may interact with certain medications, such as antidepressants and blood pressure drugs, leading to adverse reactions. Therefore, it's essential to consult with a healthcare provider before using evodia for medicinal purposes.
precautions_desc: You should take some precautions when using Evodia rutaecarpa for medicinal purposes, such as strictly adhering to recommended dosages to avoid potential toxicity. It's crucial to consult with a healthcare professional before starting any new herbal remedy, especially for pregnant women, as evodia may stimulate uterine contractions. Additionally, individuals with gastrointestinal issues should exercise caution, as evodia can increase gastric acid secretion, potentially exacerbating symptoms. Furthermore, those taking medication for hypertension should consult their healthcare provider, as evodia may interact with certain antihypertensive drugs. Lastly, it's essential to purchase evodia from a reputable source to ensure product quality and safety.
history_desc: Evodia rutaecarpa, also known as Wu Zhu Yu in traditional Chinese medicine, has a long history of use for medicinal purposes dating back thousands of years. The fruit of the Evodia tree contains active compounds, including evodiamine, which has been shown to have various pharmacological effects, such as anti-inflammatory, analgesic, and anti-cancer properties. In traditional Chinese medicine, Evodia rutaecarpa has been used to treat a variety of ailments, including gastrointestinal disorders, headaches, and menstrual pain. The herb is often combined with other herbs to create customized formulations based on the individual's specific needs.

In Chinese mythology, Evodia rutaecarpa is associated with several deities and legends. One legend tells the story of the goddess Xi Wangmu, who is said to have discovered the Evodia tree while wandering in the mountains. She found the fruit to be bitter, but also invigorating, and decided to use it as a remedy for her ailments. Another legend tells the story of the Eight Immortals, a group of Taoist sages who were said to have achieved immortality through the use of various herbs and elixirs. One of the Immortals, Lan Caihe, is often depicted holding a branch of Evodia rutaecarpa, symbolizing its importance in Taoist philosophy and practices.

Evodia rutaecarpa has been used in various religious and spiritual rituals throughout history. In Taoist practices, the herb is often burned as incense during meditation and prayer, as it is believed to help purify the mind and body. In some Chinese temples, Evodia rutaecarpa is used in the preparation of offerings to the gods, as it is believed to bring good fortune and protection. In traditional Chinese weddings, the bride and groom may drink a tea made from Evodia rutaecarpa, as it is believed to bring happiness and fertility to the couple.

Evodia rutaecarpa has been mentioned in various literary works throughout history. In the famous Chinese novel "Journey to the West," the monk Tripitaka and his companions encounter a group of demons who try to poison them with a soup made from Evodia rutaecarpa. However, the monk is able to detect the poison and avoids eating the soup. In traditional Chinese poetry, Evodia rutaecarpa is often used as a metaphor for bitterness and hardship, as well as resilience and perseverance. The herb's ability to withstand harsh conditions and produce powerful medicinal compounds has made it a symbol of strength and determination in Chinese culture.

Evodia rutaecarpa has been associated with various symbolic meanings throughout history. The herb's bitter taste and spicy aroma have made it a symbol of resilience and perseverance, as it is able to thrive in harsh conditions and produce powerful medicinal compounds. In Chinese culture, Evodia rutaecarpa is often associated with the concept of "yang," which represents masculinity, heat, and energy. The herb's ability to stimulate the body's circulation and metabolism has made it a symbol of vitality and strength. Additionally, Evodia rutaecarpa is often used in Chinese feng shui practices to bring positive energy and protection to a space, as it is believed to ward off negative influences and promote harmony and balance.
history_intro: Evodia rutaecarpa has several historical medicinal uses, such as treating gastrointestinal disorders and promoting blood circulation. In Chinese mythology, evodia is considered a symbol of longevity and is often used in ceremonies to ensure a long and healthy life. Ancient rituals involving evodia include burning the plant's leaves to ward off evil spirits and promote good health. In traditional Chinese medicine literature, evodia is often mentioned for its ability to treat various ailments, including stomach pain and inflammation. The symbolism of evodia extends beyond its medicinal uses, as it is also seen as a symbol of prosperity and abundance in Chinese culture.
botany_intro: Evodia rutaecarpa belongs to the Rutaceae family, in the genus Evodia. This species is a deciduous tree or shrub that can grow up to 10 meters tall, featuring compound, odd-pinnate leaves and small, greenish-white flowers. Two common variants of Evodia rutaecarpa are E. rutaecarpa var. rutaecarpa and E. rutaecarpa var. officinalis, differing mainly in fruit shape and size. Evodia rutaecarpa is native to central and southern China, typically found in mountainous regions and forested valleys. The life cycle of Evodia rutaecarpa involves flowering from June to July, followed by fruiting from August to October. The fruit, a capsule, splits into 3 parts when ripe, releasing numerous small, black seeds.
horticulture_intro: Evodia rutaecarpa needs full sun to partial shade and well-drained soil to thrive. When planting, ensure that you provide it with enough space to grow, as it can reach up to 20 feet tall and 15 feet wide. During the growing season, water it regularly, keeping the soil moist but not soggy. Harvest the fruit when it turns bright red and begins to soften. Evodia rutaecarpa is generally pest and disease-free, but it can be susceptible to scale insects and verticillium wilt. To prevent these issues, keep the area around the plant clean and free of debris, and avoid overwatering.
